era_context: The Eastern Roman Empire under Zeno attempts to resolve the Christological schism between Monophysite and Chalcedonian factions through imperial decree. In China, the Northern Wei Dynasty under the Xiaowen Emperor pursues aggressive Sinicization. In the West, Germanic kingdoms consolidate rule over former Roman territories. This is a period of religious ferment, political centralization, and the transformation of late antique institutions into early medieval forms.

events.title: Issuance of the Henotikon (Edict of Union)
events.description: Emperor Zeno issues the Henotikon (Edict of Union) in an attempt to reconcile the Monophysite and Chalcedonian factions of Christianity. The edict affirms the Council of Nicaea and the Council of Constantinople but avoids explicit endorsement of the Council of Chalcedon (451), which Monophysites rejected. The edict condemns both Nestorius and Eutyches, attempting a middle path. However, the compromise satisfies neither faction and deepens the schism, leading to the Acacian Schism (484–519) between Constantinople and Rome.
key_figures: Zeno (Emperor of the Eastern Roman Empire)
Acacius of Constantinople (Patriarch, who drafted the edict)
Pope Felix III (who rejected the edict)
events.certainty_note: The Henotikon is well-attested in multiple sources and is dated to 482 with high confidence. It is one of the most significant religious documents of the late 5th century.

events.title: Xiaowen Emperor's Sinicization Edict
events.description: The Xiaowen Emperor issues edicts promoting Sinicization within the Northern Wei Dynasty. These edicts mandate the adoption of Han Chinese surnames by the Tuoba nobility, promote the use of Han Chinese language in the court, and encourage Han Chinese cultural practices. The edicts are part of a broader reform program aimed at integrating the Tuoba-ruled state with Han Chinese civilization and strengthening central authority. These reforms provoke resistance from conservative Tuoba aristocrats.

sources.name: Wei Shu (Book of Wei), compiled by Sima Guang
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The Sinicization edicts are well-attested in Chinese historiography and are dated to the early 480s. The specific year 482 is assigned by convention, though some sources place them slightly earlier or later.

events.title: Ostrogothic Kingdom Consolidation in Italy
events.description: The Ostrogothic Kingdom, established by Theodoric the Great (r. 493–526), is in its early phase of consolidation. By 482, Theodoric is still in the Balkans, but the groundwork for his invasion of Italy is being laid. The Eastern Roman Empire under Zeno is preparing to support Theodoric's invasion as a means of removing the Visigothic threat and restoring imperial control over Italy. This period marks the transition from Odoacer's rule to Theodoric's.
events.certainty_note: Theodoric's invasion of Italy occurred in 493, not 482. The year 482 is assigned as a marker of the political preparations and negotiations that preceded the invasion. No specific event is dated to 482.

disconfirming_evidence: The Henotikon is well-attested and dated to 482 with high confidence. However, its significance is sometimes overstated: it did not immediately cause the Acacian Schism, which developed gradually over the following years. The schism was not formally declared until 484. The Xiaowen Emperor's Sinicization edicts are well-documented but the specific year 482 is assigned by convention; some sources place them in 481 or 483. Theodoric's invasion of Italy is dated to 493, not 482, though preparations were underway in 482.
historiographic_note: The year 482 is moderately well-documented, primarily due to the Henotikon, which is attested in multiple sources. Byzantine sources (Theophanes, Evagrius, Procopius) provide detailed accounts of the religious crisis. Chinese sources (Wei Shu, Zizhi Tongjian) document the Sinicization reforms. However, these sources are later compilations, not contemporary records. Western European sources are minimal, reflecting the fragmentation of literacy after the Western Roman collapse. The historical record for 482 is biased toward religious and political matters in the Mediterranean and China; other regions are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Sinicization reforms lead to capital relocation
